literal: strand, cord, constituent (root sense: a strand of a rope); in later technical usage 'quality, attribute', but the Sāṃkhya register preserves the strand-of-prakṛti sense and the Vaiśeṣika register the property-of-substance sense — these are different metaphysical registers that English flattens
invariant_definition:
A *guṇa* is not 'a quality' in the Western analytic sense of an accidental predicate inhering in a substance. Two distinct technical registers must be held apart, because the Vedāntic schools work primarily within the first while continuing to read texts that operate in the second.

(1) **Sāṃkhya register (the dominant Vedāntic register).** The three *guṇas* — *sattva* (lucid, ordered, illuminating), *rajas* (active, energizing, passion-producing), *tamas* (heavy, obscuring, inertial) — are the three ontological strands or constituent fabrics of *prakṛti* itself. They are not properties *of* the world; they are what the world is made of. *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 11–13 describes their joint operation under the rope-strand metaphor: the manifest is woven from three strands that mutually suppress and support one another, the dominance-ratio at any locus determining the character of that locus. The *guṇas* are *bhautika* in a strict sense — substantial constituents, not adjectives. This is why *guṇa-traya*, *traiguṇya*, *guṇātīta*, *nirguṇa*, *saguṇa* all carry weight that 'three qualities', 'tri-quality', 'beyond qualities', 'without qualities', 'with qualities' cannot bear: the Sanskrit terms reach into the constitutive metaphysics of *prakṛti*, while the English glosses suggest mere predicate-logic.

(2) **Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika register.** *Guṇa* is one of the six (or seven) *padārtha*s of Vaiśeṣika categorial ontology — a real but dependent property-substance inhering by *samavāya* in *dravya*. Here 'quality' is a defensible English gloss because the Vaiśeṣika grid of *padārtha*s is itself a property-of-substance scheme. The twenty-four *guṇa*s of classical Vaiśeṣika (color, taste, smell, touch, number, magnitude, separateness, conjunction, disjunction, priority, posteriority, weight, fluidity, viscidity, sound, *buddhi*, *sukha*, *duḥkha*, *icchā*, *dveṣa*, *prayatna*, *dharma*, *adharma*, *saṃskāra*) are inhering properties, not constitutive strands.

In Vedānta the two registers are constantly in play. When a school says *brahman* is *nirguṇa*, the question 'in which register?' is doctrinally decisive — see the entries on *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa* for the sub-school analysis.

per_school.definition: At the *vyāvahārika* level, Advaita treats *prakṛti* as *triguṇātmikā* and distinguishes *māyā* from *avidyā* through the purity and impurity of *sattva* within that same guṇic matrix [[Pañcadaśī 1.15-16](cite://vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.15-16)]. In Madhusūdana's devotional Advaita, hearing a text woven from the grandeur of Bhagavān's *guṇas* is the direct *sādhana* of *bhakti*, whereas the *mahāvākya* is the *sādhana* of *brahma-vidyā* [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1)] [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā.6](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.6)].

Rāmānuja takes *brahman* to denote *Puruṣottama*, whose *svarūpa* is intrinsically free from all *doṣa* and constituted by an unsurpassed, innumerable host of *kalyāṇa-guṇas*; even the lexical force of *brahman* is explained through greatness in *svarūpa* and in *guṇa* [[Śrī Bhāṣya 1.1.1, comm. 6-8](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6)] [[Śrī Bhāṣya 1.1.1, comm. 7](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/7)] [[Śrī Bhāṣya 1.1.1, comm. 8](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/8)]. The *jīva* is described as *jñānānandaika-guṇa*, while the *antaryāmin* Nārāyaṇa is the *anavadhikātiśayāsaṃkhyeya-kalyāṇa-guṇagaṇa* who qualifies and governs the whole *cid-acid*-complex by *sāmānādhikaraṇya* and inner-selfhood [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)] [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

Madhva's school treats Viṣṇu as *sarvaguṇa-pūrṇa* and *doṣa-varjita*, and explicitly refuses any separation of divine *guṇa* from divine *svarūpa*: *guṇa-kriyādayo viṣṇoḥ svarūpam* [[BBsBh 1.1.1.1](cite://madhva/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.1.1)] [[VTV 3.12](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.12)]. The Lord's displayed embodiment never compromises those pure auspicious *guṇas* [[VTV 3.9](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.9)]. The liberated *jīva* retains *cid-ānanda*-beginning *guṇas* only by *sādṛśya*, so *abheda*-language marks likeness and dependence, never *svarūpābheda* with Īśvara [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)] [[Anuvy. 2.3.67](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.67)].

Jīva Gosvāmin construes the *jīva*-multitude as standing, by Bhagavān's *svābhāvika-tad-acintya-śakti*, in a relation of *guṇa-sthānīyatva* to him, like ray-atoms to the sun; *abheda-śāstra* therefore requires simultaneous preservation of *vyatireka* and *avyatireka* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Bhagavān alone is the final *prayojana* because he is *sarvādhika-guṇa-śālin*, the unsurpassed locus of supreme qualities and thus of *parama-prema* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Nimbārka's meditation-verse defines the supreme Brahman as Kṛṣṇa who is by nature devoid of all *doṣa* and the single mass of inexhaustible *kalyāṇa-guṇas* [[Daśa-Ślokī 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)]. The Lord's *brahmatva*, *vyūha*-form, and auspicious *guṇa*-plenitude are presented together as one indivisible object of contemplation [[Daśa-Ślokī 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)].

For Sāṃkhya, *guṇa* names the constitutive strands of *prakṛti* itself, not adventitious predicates of an underlying substrate: the manifest and the unmanifest are both *triguṇa*, whereas *puruṣa* is their contrary [[SK 11-14](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/11-14)]. The guṇas are internally dynamic powers marked by mutual overpowering, support, generation, and coupling, and they are specified as *sattva*, *rajas*, and *tamas* through the functions of illumination, activity, and obscuration [[SK 12-13](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/11-14)]. Because the effect is constituted by the guṇas of its cause, the whole evolute series is *traiguṇya*; liberation therefore depends on discriminating the *aguṇa* puruṣa for whose sake guṇa-endowed prakṛti continues to operate [[SK 14](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/11-14)] [[SK 60](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/60)].

Yoga inherits the Sāṃkhya sense of *guṇa* but places it directly within the discipline of liberation: higher dispassion is defined as *guṇavaitṛṣṇya*, indifference to the whole guṇic field once *puruṣa-khyāti* has arisen [[YS 1.16](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.16)]. The dṛśya is the triguṇa complex itself, articulated as *prakāśa-kriyā-sthiti* and unfolded through the stages called the *guṇaparvāṇi* [[YS 2.18-19](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.18-19)] [[YBh on YS 2.18](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18)]. Kaivalya is therefore the *pratiprasava* of the guṇas when they have become purposeless for puruṣa, not an annihilation of consciousness but its non-mixture with guṇic process [[YS 4.32-34](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.32-34)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *guṇa* is a dependent *padārtha*: it resides in a substance, is itself *aguṇavat*, and is not a cause of conjunction and disjunction [[VS 1.1.15](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.15)]. Nyāya applies the category through its account of the elements and the senses, treating smell, taste, color, touch, and sound as the guṇas that function as objective properties of earth and the other elemental substrates [[NS 1.1.14](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.14)]. Vātsyāyana extends the same categorial logic to psychical features such as desire, holding that these too are guṇas and that their dravya-āśraya is the self [[NB p.156-157](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/p.156-157)].

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *guṇa* is not a free-standing metaphysical quality-category but a subordinate ritual accessory whose very nature is *parārtha* with respect to a principal act [[MS 1.4.16](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.4.16)]. Accordingly, a *guṇavidhi* does not automatically carry further dharmas from one occurrence to another; it ordinarily enjoins only that accessory, precisely because the accessory stands on a level coordinate with the competing occurrence under dispute [[MS 7.3.17](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/7.3.17)]. When Jaimini says *guṇānām abhidhānatvāt*, the point is hermeneutic: accessory-features can function as denotative delimiters of the intended performance because they are dependent qualifiers of the principal sacrifice rather than autonomous ritual ends [[MS 7.3.34](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/7.3.34)].

Mādhyamaka does not admit *guṇa* as an ultimately real category distinct from the thing qualified; after the sevenfold chariot-analysis, parts, wholes, qualities, and quality-bearers are all unfound as *svabhāva*-siddha entities [[MAv 6.167](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.167)]. Guṇa-language is therefore retained only at the level of *lokaprasiddhi*: ordinary discourse may speak of qualities and bearers, but ultimate analysis dissolves both into dependent designation [[MAv 6.167](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.167)].

In the Yogācāra epistemological wing, *guṇa* is not an independently apprehended property inhering in a separate substratum; Dharmakīrti argues that what is taken as *guṇa* and *pradhāna*, or as *viśeṣaṇa* and *viśeṣya*, depends on conceptual orientation within a single cognition [[PV 2.226-229](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.226-229)]. Hence no further *guṇin* needs to be posited behind the appearing feature-set, and attempts to explain error by attaching an additional guṇa merely redescribe the cognitive construction rather than disclose an independently given object-structure [[PV 2.228-229](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.226-229)] [[PV 3.44-45](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.44-45)].

Trika accepts a *guṇa*-level within the lower tattva-cascade, placing the *guṇa-saṃjñita* domain below *buddhi* and above the manifest evolutes of *prakṛti* [[TA 8.253-254](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.253-259)]. Abhinavagupta nevertheless criticizes the Sāṃkhya account of a self-originating disturbance of guṇa-equilibrium, insisting that the kṣobha of prakṛti is grounded in the will of the free Lord rather than in guṇas operating autonomously [[TA 8.255-259](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.253-259)]. Accordingly, *avyakta*, *prakṛti*, *pradhāna*, and even *guṇakāraṇa* are treated as names within the māyā-born lower ontology, not as an ultimate explanatory principle independent of Śiva's *svātantrya* [[TA 8.272-274](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8.272-274)].

For Jainism, substance is intrinsically *guṇa-paryāyavat*: *dravya* is never a bare substrate but always accompanied by attributes and modes [[TS 5.37](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.37-39)]. Guṇas depend on *dravya*, yet are not themselves bearers of further guṇas, and change is named *pariṇāma* as the becoming of just those dependent determinations [[TS 5.38-39](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.37-39)].

translator_note: Translating *guṇa* uniformly as 'quality' is the foundational lazy collapse from which a chain of downstream errors follows. The Sāṃkhya-derived register — where *guṇa* names a constitutive strand of *prakṛti* — is the dominant Vedāntic register. In that register *guṇa* is no more a 'quality' than a strand of a rope is a 'quality' of the rope. When the gloss 'quality' is then carried into *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa*, the resulting English ('without qualities', 'with qualities') reads as a flat predicate-logic claim and severs the term from the metaphysics it lives in. Acceptable practice: leave *guṇa* untranslated; on first use specify the register (Sāṃkhya-strand vs. Vaiśeṣika-padārtha vs. Vaiṣṇava-kalyāṇa); never translate *guṇātīta* as 'beyond qualities' (use 'beyond the three *guṇa*s' or 'beyond the strands of *prakṛti*'); never gloss *nirguṇa* as 'qualityless' or *saguṇa* as 'qualified' without qualification.
